MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. WALTER A. HAYNES, IV, Esq. of Albert G. Stoll, Jr., A Law Corporation (Tel: 415-576-1500) for plaintiff;
Atty. MARGUERITE E. MEADE, Esq. of List Jacobson-Kwok Thorndal, LLP (Tel: 510-302-6600) for defendant John Stephenson.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF ELOY ROBLES' Motion To Seal Court Records.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "motion to seal court records; in the alterative limit public access to the case records via the court's website; convert the case name to a pseudonym to further advance the anonymity of parties in the public domain after confidential settlement and/or in the alternative seal portions of the court record on privacy" is denied.

When plaintiff filed this suit 32 months ago, he considered using a pseudonym but elected not to, hoping his true name would cause others to "come forward with similar claims." (Mot. 5:1-4.) As request by the parties, the court entered a protective order that permitted confidentiality designations. (Id. at 5:8-10.) As plaintiff reports, there were "numerous discovery disputes" and a summary judgment motion. (Id. at 4:10-12.) However, defendant asserts (Opp. 2:4-3:14.) - and plaintiff does not dispute - that, during the 32 months of litigation, plaintiff never designated anything confidential under the protective order and never sought to seal anything submitted to the court. The parties recently entered a confidential settlement (not in the court record) and defendant says he has performed under it. (Opp. 3:15-21.) This motion followed.

As defendant cites, "court records are presumed open." (CRC 2.550(c); see also Estate of Hearst (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 777, 782-83 ("court records are public records").) Plaintiff cites no authority for tactically suing under his own name and litigating for 32 months without any effort at confidentiality, but then seeking it after the case settles. As plaintiff concedes, "the genie is out of the bottle." (Mot. 4:13.)

Order signed.
